title: Root Number Correlation Bias of Fourier Coefficients of Modular Forms
speaker: Nina Zubrilina
abstract: In a recent machine learning based study, He, Lee, Oliver, and Pozdnyakov observed a striking oscillating pattern in the average value of the P-th Frobenius trace of elliptic curves of prescribed rank and conductor in an interval range. Sutherland discovered that this bias extends to Dirichlet coefficients of a much broader class of arithmetic L-functions when split by root number.

proof_stages.label: Empirical root-number bias and conductor scaling
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from machine-learning and elliptic-curve computations showing that averages of Frobenius traces over conductor windows oscillate according to root number, become scale-invariant in p/N on dyadic conductor intervals, and disappear under other orderings.

proof_stages.label: Modular-form limiting problem and short-interval theorem
proof_stages.summary: The empirical phenomenon is reformulated for all newforms of squarefree levels, leading to a local short-interval theorem asserting that the normalized average of a_f(p) times the root number converges to an explicit square-root limiting function, with dyadic averages recovered by integration.

proof_stages.label: Trace-formula reduction to Hurwitz class numbers
proof_stages.summary: The root-number-weighted Hecke-coefficient sum is identified, up to sign, with the trace of T_p composed with an Atkin-Lehner operator, and the relevant trace formula expresses it as a finite sum of Hurwitz class numbers plus an elementary term.

proof_stages.label: Evaluation of the easy class-number terms
proof_stages.summary: The terms H(-pN) and H(-4pN) are handled by the class-number formula, truncating the L-series, switching the order of summation, and using the square/nonsquare character dichotomy with Perron/convexity estimates to obtain a main term with power-saving error.

proof_stages.label: Congruence analysis for hard discriminants
proof_stages.summary: For the remaining terms H(k^2N^2-4pN), the proof introduces square divisors and carefully tracks divisibility and mod-four constraints, then splits the resulting sums into small-parameter, large-auxiliary-index, and large-square-divisor ranges.

proof_stages.label: Small-parameter main term from squarefree equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: In the range where the auxiliary index and square divisor are small, equidistribution of squarefree integers in congruence classes modulo the required moduli converts the sums into explicit local-density sums, which become multiplicative Euler-product constants.

proof_stages.label: Error bounds for the complementary ranges
proof_stages.summary: When the auxiliary index is large but the square divisor is small, Poisson summation in arithmetic progressions gives a saving depending on the squarefree kernel, while for large square divisor a trivial class-number bound suffices; after summing, these ranges are negligible.

proof_stages.label: Extraction and qualitative behavior of the limiting function
proof_stages.summary: Combining the main terms yields the explicit limiting function, whose square-root breakpoints explain its non-differentiability and suggest quadratic-scale self-similarity, repeated sign changes, and recovery of the original dyadic curve by integration.

examples.name: Fixed-prime single-class-number regime
examples.note: When p is fixed and the level grows, the trace formula has essentially one class-number term, illustrating how earlier square-root root-number biases are visible in the formula.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the fixed-prime regime in the trace formula, noting that only one class-number term remains and using class-number bounds to see the excess size.

passes.reason: The fixed-prime limit is a boundary regime in which the trace-formula sum collapses to a single class-number term.
